source_title: Morgan Housel: Wealth is What You Have Minus What You Want

paragraphs.speaker: morgan
paragraphs.name: Morgan Housel
paragraphs.text: It's not necessarily how much you have. It's just a contrast to what you have before. Would you rather have a net worth of a million dollars when you used to have two million, or would you rather have a net worth of 500,000 when you used to have 200,000? And psychologically, most people would rather have 500,000. The speed at which a luxury becomes a necessity is two seconds.

paragraphs.speaker: morgan
paragraphs.name: Morgan Housel
paragraphs.text: Affordable housing, I think, is the single biggest social problem because so many other social problems that might seem bigger than that are downstream of housing. A lot of the drug problem, the fertility crisis, the degradation of politics, because if you don't feel like you're invested in your community or you're invested in your country, it's much easier to be like, burn the place down. If you have to sum up doing well financially in one word, I think it's survival.

paragraphs.speaker: morgan
paragraphs.name: Morgan Housel
paragraphs.text: What has driven me are people like you and others, James Clear, Michael Lewis, people who I've really looked up to. I think there's a difference between envy and aspiration. You can be really inspired by someone's success without envying them. I can also name half a dozen people I've envied. It's usually because they achieved a level of success, but I didn't like how they did it. I've really liked looking up to people where it's like, not only do I appreciate what you did professionally, but I like the whole package of the life that you're living.

paragraphs.speaker: morgan
paragraphs.name: Morgan Housel
paragraphs.text: Nothing is a bigger motivator in life than having a couple of people that you really don't want to disappoint. I really desperately do not want to disappoint my wife and kids. I really don't want to disappoint my parents. I can have a very split personality of on one hand, I can say, man, I'm really good at what I do and I'm achieving a lot and this is amazing. And then I can very quickly flip a switch and say I'm a nobody and I've done some really bad work lately. On balance, it's a pretty healthy personality.

paragraphs.speaker: morgan
paragraphs.name: Morgan Housel
paragraphs.text: On average, people who have more money tend to have fewer bad days, but I don't know if they have more better days. I think money can be more like a vaccine where it can prevent a lot of misery. But maybe that's a good analogy because you and I don't wake up in the morning being like, oh, so glad I don't have polio. I think what people actually want to get to is contentment, which is not happiness. You'd want to get to a point where you're just like, I'm good. I'm very grateful for everything that I have and I have everything that I need and most of what I want.

paragraphs.speaker: morgan
paragraphs.name: Morgan Housel
paragraphs.text: When you daydream about having more success or more money or more stuff, by and large, what you are doing is imagining yourself having those things and being totally content with them. More often what happens is if you are in that house, you're like, oh, man, look, my neighbor, like their yard's a little nicer, isn't it? The lack of contentment is the seed of progress. So it's not a bad thing. But you can see in the individual life where if you feel like there's a hole in your soul and you just want more, the idea that if I just keep shoveling money in that hole, then I'll get to a place where I'm good—it's actually very difficult to do that.

paragraphs.speaker: morgan
paragraphs.name: Morgan Housel
paragraphs.text: Evolution cares about survival of the species, not the happiness of the individual. And the other thing with evolution is it doesn't matter how much money I have. What matters is that I have more than you. Even if the size of my house by historical standards is enormous, if my neighbor's is bigger, then suddenly it feels tiny. You can easily imagine a world where our kids and our grandkids have technology and abundance that you and I cannot fathom, and they won't feel any better for it. The speed at which a luxury becomes a necessity is two seconds.

paragraphs.speaker: shane
paragraphs.name: Shane Parrish
paragraphs.text: I remember when I was 17 and I overheard a conversation with my parents and their financial advisor. They were trying to decide whether to fix the roof or fix the car and they couldn't afford to do both. I remember just thinking in that moment, I never want to be in this situation where I have to choose between two necessary things that I have to fix and I have no money.
